explicit_text: My name is Madeleine Oudowu Odutayo, born in Houston, Texas in 2000 to Nigerian immigrant parents. Growing up, I experienced the challenges of my father’s deportation in 2004 when I was just four years old, which deeply affected my family. Despite the hardships, my mother worked tirelessly to raise me and my siblings while pursuing her nursing education. Our family’s resilience and commitment to education have shaped who I am today.

explicit_text: Nigeria is the country my parents originally came from before immigrating to the United States. After my father was deported in 2004, he had to return to Nigeria and was unable to come back for eleven years. During that time, I remember sending letters hoping they would reach him there. Nigeria represents both our roots and the place that separated our family for over a decade.

explicit_text: The United States is where I was born and raised, and where my parents sought better opportunities for our family. My mother came here alone in 1985 as a teenager, and my father joined her in 1988. We lived in Maryland and later moved to Houston, Texas, where I was born in 2000. Despite the challenges, including my father’s deportation, the US has been the backdrop for my family’s struggles and successes.

explicit_text: Houston, Texas is where I was born and where my family settled in 1997 after moving from Maryland. It’s the city where I grew up and where my parents worked hard to provide for me and my siblings. Our family was happy there until my father was deported in 2004, which changed everything. Houston holds many memories of my early childhood and the resilience of my family during difficult times.

explicit_text: Prince George's County, Maryland is where my parents met in 1989 after immigrating to the United States. They got married there and started our family, having my twin siblings in 1996 before moving to Houston in 1997. Maryland was the place where our family began and where my parents built their early life together in America.
